description: Unified dataset for blind code review study. Each question contains 4 solutions per language (2 human, 2 AI). _hidden fields must never be exposed to study participants.
uiNote: Solution order must be randomized per participant and per question. Slot labels A/B/C/D are purely positional. The _hidden key on every solution object must not be rendered in any participant-facing UI component.

questions.title: Squares of a Sorted Array
questions.summary: Given an integer array sorted in non-decreasing order (which may contain negative numbers), return a new array containing the square of each element, also so....
questions.paraphrasedPrompt: Given an integer array sorted in non-decreasing order (which may contain negative numbers), return a new array containing the square of each element, also sorted in non-decreasing order.
constraints: nums is sorted in non-decreasing order

_hidden.styleNotes: Generator inside sorted(); single expression; O(n log n)
_hidden.selectionNotes: One-line naive â€” tests whether participants accept simplicity over O(n) optimality

_hidden.styleNotes: Two-pointer from both ends filling result from right; O(n); abs() comparison
_hidden.selectionNotes: Optimal O(n) approach â€” makes the two-pointer logic explicit

solutions.code:
class Solution:
    def sortedSquares(self, nums: list[int]) -> list[int]:
        n = len(nums)
        result = [0] * n
        left, right = 0, n - 1
        position = n - 1
        while left <= right:
            left_sq = nums[left] ** 2
            right_sq = nums[right] ** 2
            if left_sq > right_sq:
                result[position] = left_sq
                left += 1
            else:
                result[position] = right_sq
                right -= 1
            position -= 1
        return result

_hidden.styleNotes: Precomputed lSq/rSq; aligned else with whitespace; O(n) two-pointer
_hidden.selectionNotes: Efficient Java â€” named square variables make comparison clear

_hidden.styleNotes: Square all elements then Arrays.sort; O(n log n) naive approach
_hidden.selectionNotes: Simple and readable â€” good for studying whether participants penalize suboptimal complexity

_hidden.styleNotes: In-place square then qsort with comparator; standard C sort pattern
_hidden.selectionNotes: Canonical C sort approach â€” comparator is a common pattern in C tutorial code

_hidden.styleNotes: O(n) two-pointer with named ls/rs; fills result from end
_hidden.selectionNotes: Efficient C â€” avoids sort, named square variables

_hidden.styleNotes: STL transform with lambda + sort; functional pipeline style
_hidden.selectionNotes: Idiomatic modern C++ â€” lambda + algorithm composition

_hidden.styleNotes: Two-pointer with abs() comparison; for-loop counting down; explicit blocks
_hidden.selectionNotes: Explicit C++ two-pointer â€” abs() makes comparison intent clear
